sound_type: sharp chip alarm -- single, sharp, high-pitched chip, percussive alarm note repeated at intervals, E=4, forest edge habitat
ecosystem: forest edge, rocky clearings, open coniferous forest, montane meadow margins. Elevation 1,000-3,500 meters. The interface between forest and open ground. Returns from grassland (78) to forest edge.
season: active spring through autumn (April-October in most populations); hibernates through winter. True hibernator with food caches. 5-7 months dormancy.

species_pair.pair_type: Sciuridae QUINTET -- FIFTH consecutive Sciuridae (75-76-77-78-79). Matches Mustelidae quintet (63-67) and Swallow quintet (26-30). THREE-WAY TIE for longest family run. Three marmots (Marmota, 75-77) + two ground squirrels (Urocitellus 78, Callospermophilus 79). Five acoustic signatures. Five habitats.

coordinates.description: Reference for forest edge habitat in the Cascade Range, Washington. Open coniferous forest, rocky clearings, montane meadow margins. The Golden-mantled Ground Squirrel inhabits the forest-meadow interface.

taxonomy_note.description: FIFTH CONSECUTIVE SCIURIDAE (75-76-77-78-79). Sciuridae quintet. Ties Mustelidae (63-67) and Swallows (26-30). NEW GENUS Callospermophilus -- third ground squirrel genus. Eighteenth mammalian entry. Sixteenth mammalian species. Sixth Rodentia. Sciuridae's fifth entry.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Callospermophilus from Greek kalos ('beautiful') + spermophilus ('seed-lover'). lateralis from Latin 'of the side,' referring to lateral body stripes. Described by Say (1823). Formerly in Spermophilus, split to Callospermophilus in 2009.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Callospermophilus -- NEW GENUS in the engine. Third ground squirrel genus after Marmota (75-77) and Urocitellus (78). 'Beautiful seed-lovers.'
taxonomy_note.order_note: Rodentia -- sixth Rodentia in the engine. Beaver (61-62), Yellow-bellied (75), Hoary (76), Olympic (77), Townsend's (78), Golden-mantled (79).
taxonomy_note.class_note: Mammalia -- eighteenth mammalian entry. Sixteenth mammalian species. Four orders. Nine families. Two infraclasses.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Sciuridae's FIFTH entry at degree 79. Fifth consecutive (75-76-77-78-79). Sciuridae quintet ties Mustelidae quintet (63-67) and Swallow quintet (26-30) for longest family run. THREE-WAY TIE.

song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. ALARM CHIP. Single sharp percussive note. Repeated at 1-3 second intervals when alarmed.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: 0.05-0.15 seconds per chip. Individual notes are very brief -- sharp onset, rapid decay.
song_metrics.note_structure: Single emphatic note -- one sharp chip per vocalization. Repeated at intervals rather than in rapid bursts (contrast Townsend's trill at 78).
song_metrics.note_types: Alarm chip (primary, sharp percussive), contact call (softer chirps), distress squeal.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Simple: single note repeated at intervals. The simplest vocal structure in the Sciuridae quintet -- one note, spaced repetition.
song_metrics.timbre: Sharp, percussive, cutting. A single emphatic pop of sound. Cuts through ambient forest noise. Higher frequency than marmot calls.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Moderate. Audible at 50-150 meters through forest edge habitat. Less open propagation than grassland (78) but the sharp frequency cuts through foliage.
song_metrics.tempo: Moderate. Individual chips at 1-3 second intervals. Slower than Townsend's trill but more emphatic per note.
song_metrics.context: ALARM. Predator detection. Less colonial than Townsend's -- the alarm serves individual warning plus loose neighbor alert.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. Alarm chip is consistent across the species' range.
song_metrics.key_property: SHARP CHIP ALARM -- the 80th acoustic element. Single sharp high-pitched chip. E=4. Percussive, emphatic. Forest edge alarm. Repeated at intervals.

physical_description.overall: Medium-small ground squirrel. 230-295 mm total length. 170-300 g. Distinctive lateral body stripes (white bordered by black) but NO facial stripes (key chipmunk distinction). Golden-brown 'mantle' across head and shoulders.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 79: (1) LATERAL BODY STRIPES -- white bordered by black, similar to chipmunks. (2) NO FACIAL STRIPES -- the key distinction from chipmunks. (3) GOLDEN MANTLE -- golden-brown wash across head and shoulders. (4) LARGER THAN CHIPMUNKS -- 170-300 g vs. 30-70 g. (5) FOREST EDGE HABITAT -- rocky clearings, open conifer forest.
physical_description.fur_detail: BODY: Gray-brown to reddish-brown. Two prominent lateral stripes: white (central) bordered by black. HEAD AND SHOULDERS: Golden-brown 'mantle' -- the namesake feature. BELLY: Lighter, buff to white. FACE: Clean, unstriped -- the critical chipmunk distinction.
physical_description.dental_detail: Rodent dentition. Granivorous/omnivorous. Seeds, nuts, berries, fungi, insects.
physical_description.field_id: In forest edge or rocky clearing: a ground squirrel with chipmunk-like body stripes BUT no facial stripes. Golden-brown head/shoulders. Larger than chipmunks. Sharp chip alarm when disturbed. Often seen sitting erect on rocks or logs at forest margins.

nodes.title: Sciuridae Quintet (75-76-77-78-79) -- Three-Way Tie
nodes.description: Five consecutive Sciuridae. Matches Mustelidae (63-67) and Swallows (26-30). Three marmots (75-77) + two ground squirrels (78-79). Five acoustic signatures. Five habitats. THREE-WAY TIE for longest family run.

nodes.title: Sharp Chip Alarm -- 80th Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Single sharp high-pitched chip. 4,000-9,000 Hz. E=4. Percussive, emphatic. Repeated at 1-3 second intervals. Cuts through forest sound. Forest edge alarm.

nodes.title: Forest Edge Habitat -- Returns from Grassland
nodes.description: Forest edge, rocky clearings, open coniferous forest. 1,000-3,500 m. Returns from pure grassland (78) to forest interface. Each quintet member in a different habitat.

nodes.title: New Genus: Callospermophilus
nodes.description: Third ground squirrel genus in the engine after Marmota (75-77) and Urocitellus (78). 'Beautiful seed-lovers.' Shift within Sciuridae quintet continues.

nodes.title: Not a Chipmunk -- No Facial Stripes
nodes.description: Often confused with chipmunks due to body stripes. Key distinction: no facial stripes. Chipmunks have facial stripes. Golden-mantled does not. Also larger (170-300 g vs. 30-70 g).

nodes.title: Forest Edge Predator-Prey Network
nodes.description: Hunted by American Marten (65, canopy-to-ground), Red Fox (71), Short-tailed Weasel (67). Forest edge brings the marten connection -- a predator that descends from canopy.

nodes.title: The Jesse Marchant Parallel
nodes.description: Both solitary (less colonial squirrel / solo artist). Both at the edge (forest edge / artistic edge). Both often miscategorized (chipmunk confusion / JBM identity). Three dimensions.

cross_references.relationship: SCIURIDAE QUINTET. Ground squirrel to ground squirrel. Grassland (78) to forest edge (79). Trill vs. chip. Colonial (78) vs. loosely aggregated (79). Two ground squirrel genera: Urocitellus and Callospermophilus.
SCIURIDAE QUINTET (75-79). Three marmots + two ground squirrels. Five acoustic signatures. Five habitats. Alpine to grassland to forest edge.
FOREST EDGE SHARED. Predator-prey. Both inhabit forest edge. The weasel hunts ground squirrels at the forest-meadow interface.
PREDATOR-PREY. Fox hunts ground squirrels at forest margins. Fox bark (71) vs. squirrel chip (79). Overlapping edge habitat.

CANOPY-TO-GROUND PREDATOR-PREY. The marten descends from conifer canopy to hunt ground squirrels at forest edge. Mustelidae quintet member (65) preys on Sciuridae quintet member (79). Cross-quintet predation.
Eighteenth mammalian entry. Sixteenth species. Sixth Rodentia. Sciuridae quintet (75-79) ties Mustelidae (63-67) and Swallows (26-30). New genus Callospermophilus.
Forest edge habitat. Interface between coniferous forest and open ground. Scatter-hoarder -- seed dispersal role. Prey base for marten, fox, weasels. Forest-meadow ecotone species.
